hours: The Assessor’s primary objective is to make sure that the property tax burden is distributed fairly among Killingworth property owners. The office assesses all taxable property located within the town according to state law and under state oversight. The assessor is also responsible for reviewing and approving various forms of property tax relief and exemptions, such as senior tax relief and veterans’ exemptions. The assessor produces the annual Grand List, a record of all taxable and tax - exempt property in Killingworth. Under state law, the Grand List must be submitted by the end of January for all property ownership on October 1 of the previous year. Property Records GIS Maps Revaluation CURRENT OFFICE OPERATIONS: The Assessor’s office is operating during normal business hours. Staff may be working in the field, or remotely, so it is important for callers to leave messages. Walk - ins are welcome, but appointments are encouraged due to the nature of our work. BOARD OF ASSESSMENT APPEALS DEADLINE: FEBRUARY 20, 2026 The Board of Assessment Appeals is accepting appeal applications for the October 1, 2025 Grand List, as well as appeals on the October 1, 2024 Supplemental Motor Vehicle List, until February 20, 2026 . Applications are available for download on this page under “Forms & Resources, ” hardcopies are available at Town Hall or can be mailed/emailed upon request. Individual hearings will be scheduled in March, dates TBD. Appellants must appear in person (or designate an agent, in writing). —————————————————————————— HOMEOWNERS AND TOTALLY DISABLED TAX RELIEF PROGRAM DEADLINE: MAY 15, 2026 Call the Assessor’s Office at (860)663 - 1765 Ext.506 to schedule an appointment to apply for this income - based tax credit after you have filed your federal income tax return. The income limits for the State program this year are $46, 300 if single, and $56, 500 if married. (This includes all taxable and non - taxable income, including Social Security). The total maximum income for the local program is $50, 000 . If you are entitled to additional veteran’s benefits, your application will be filed at the same time. Please note: Additional requirements other than income apply. Click for more information about the Home Owners Tax Relief Program 2026 Click for more information about Additional Veterans Exemptions 2026 To discuss your eligibility or schedule your appointment, contact the Assessor’s office at (860)663 - 1765 Ext 506 or by email AssessorClerk@townofkillingworth.com . Frequently Asked Questions What is an assessment? tokadmin 2022 - 07 - 23T12:32:28 - 04:00 What is an assessment? An assessment is the value placed on property appearing in the Grand List—the list of all taxable and tax - exempt property in the town. To determine the tax liability for a particular property, the property assessment is multiplied by the town’s mill rate. In Connecticut, an assessment is equal to 70 percent of the fair market value as established by the municipal assessor. For motor vehicles and business personal property, this assessment is recalculated annually as of October 1st. Real estate assessments are established during the town - wide revaluations, which are performed on a schedule established by state law. What is the fair market value, and who determines it? tokadmin 2022 - 07 - 23T12:32:11 - 04:00 What is the fair market value, and who determines it? Fair market value is a legal term defined by the courts and state statute as the purchase price that a property would bring on the open market, given prudent, knowledgeable, and willing buyers and sellers. Fair market value is the standard by which the fairness of all assessments is judged. Buyers and sellers determine the fair market value of real estate. The appraiser or assessor analyzes real estate transactions that occur within a community and determines the factors that led to the final sale prices. These analyses are then used to estimate the market values of all properties in a community. Some of the typical factors used by an appraiser in estimating market values include location, condition, age, size, and quality of improvements. What guidelines must the assessor follow? tokadmin 2022 - 07 - 23T12:31:53 - 04:00 What guidelines must the assessor follow? The assessor follows: Connecticut General Statutes (primary Title 12) relevant case law (which assists in interpreting these statutes) regulations and administrative pronouncements from the State of Connecticut Office of Policy & Management, Intergovernmental Policy Division. How is the tax rate established? tokadmin 2022 - 07 - 23T12:31:35 - 04:00 How is the tax rate established? The tax rate, or mill rate, is set annually by the elected Board of Finance members once the town’s budget has been approved in Town Meeting and the Regional School District 17 budget has been approved in a referendum. In simplest terms, the mill rate is the town’s levy—the amount of money the town needs to generate through the property tax in order to provide town and school district services—divided by the taxable grand list. In recent years, the education budget has accounted for about 80 percent of all tax dollars. How can I determine if my assessment is fair? tokadmin 2022 - 07 - 23T12:31:17 - 04:00 How can I determine if my assessment is fair? To judge the fairness of your assessment, two questions should be asked. First, does the assessment represent 70 percent of the probable selling price as of the revaluation date of October 1, 2021. Second, is my assessment similar to that of comparable properties in town as of October 1, 2021. The assessor’s office maintains information that will help you answer these questions, including a list of properties that have sold in town and the assessments of all properties in town.

The department currently has 5 full - time employees; contractors assist with seasonal work including snow removal, mowing, street sweeping, chip sealing and catch - basin cleaning. The Public Works department is on call 24/7 for town emergencies such as downed trees and collapsed roads. Report a Killingworth Public Works Issue Click to Sign Up for CT and/or Town Announcements Transfer Station Frequently Asked Questions Why use chip seal? tokadmin 2022 - 09 - 05T09:27:19 - 04:00 Why use chip seal? Chip seal provides the opportunity to maintain the roads for very low cost. A chip seal is about one fourth to one fifth the cost of a conventional asphalt overlay. By extending the time between asphalt overlays, chip seal results in lower costs over the long term. By placing a chip seal sooner than asphalt overlay would be placed, the traveling public benefits from roads maintained in better condition. Chip seals eliminate the need to crack seal. Chip seal enhances safety by providing good skid resistance. Chip seal provides an effective moisture barrier for the underlying pavement against water intrusion by sealing cracks in the pavement. Chip seal prevents deterioration of the asphalt surface from the effects of aging and oxidation due to water and sun. DOT has successfully used chip seal for over 25 years to maintain state routes. Chip seal is used only on low traffic routes, less than 2, 500 vehicles per day. Chip seal virtually eliminates black ice. In hot weather chip seal reseals cracks by flowing back together. The Snow Plow damaged my mailbox, what can be done? tokadmin 2022 - 09 - 05T09:26:43 - 04:00 The Snow Plow damaged my mailbox, what can be done? The Board of Selectmen establishes the following policy concerning mailboxes: Residents are reminded to adhere to U.S. Postal Service regulations regarding placement of mailboxes; see www.usps.com/manage/know - mailbox - guidelines . In the event a mailbox or post is physically struck by a snow plow employed by the town, the Town will provide a basic replacement mailbox and post. A mailbox or post will not be repaired or replaced if damage was caused by the force of snow thrown by plows. Who is responsible for a dead tree on my property? tokadmin 2022 - 09 - 05T09:27:02 - 04:00 Who is responsible for a dead tree on my property? Tree mortality in Connecticut is at an all time high. If you are concerned about a tree, contact the Selectmen’s office at 860 - 663 - 1765, x 501 and we will investigate. Thomas Lentz, thomas.lentz@yale.edu Open Burning Official Residents who wish to kindle an outdoor fire, other than in an outdoor grill, fireplace, or approved incinerator, must obtain a permit beforehand from the Open Burning Official. Such fires may not be within 50 feet of a building or other structure, and such burning must be conducted only between sunrise and sunset. The Open Burning Official grants or denies open burning permits in accordance with Section 22a - 174 of the Connecticut Statutes as amended, the regulations of the Department of Environmental Protection, and Killingworth Ordinances. Because of environmental and safety concerns, open burning is not encouraged. Ethan Daniel Drain, 860 - 853 - 8835 Parmelee Farm Parmelee Farm is a 130 - acre historic site located at 465 Route 81 in Killingworth. Many community activities are held there, including stonewall building workshops, annual artisan market, and the Boy Scouts’ Klondike Derby. The Parmelee Farm connects our community to the natural environment and cultural heritage of Killingworth through education, preservation, passive recreation, and the arts. For more information please visit www.parmeleefarm.org Parmelee Farm is also home to Killingworth’s Community Gardens, whose mission is to allow residents to come together for the purpose of food production, environmental education and to encourage multi - generational gatherings. Individuals and groups at all levels of gardening expertise are welcome. For more information please visit www.parmeleefarm.org/community - gardens/ Probate Court Killingworth belongs to the Saybrook Probate District, located in the Old Saybrook Town Hall, 302 Main Street, Old Saybrook, Connecticut 06475. The court is open Monday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. to 4:30 p.m. Under special circumstances, arrangements may be made to meet with the court in Killingworth. Jeannine Lewis, Judge of Probate 860 - 510 - 5028 Public Transportation River Valley Transit (RVT), a transit agency operated by the Estuary Transit District, provides transportation resources to Chester, Clinton, Deep River Durham, Essex, Killingworth, Lyme, Middlefield, Middletown, Old Lyme, Old Saybrook and Westbrook. RVT offers a range of services including Dial - A - Ride, ADA Paratransit options, fixed route and deviated fixed - route buses, as well as free connections to CTtransit and SEAT, and most are open to the general public. For more information go to rivervalleytransit.com . Social Services The Municipal Agent for the Elderly assists town residents by providing information and referrals to local, state, and federal services and benefit programs. The Municipal Agent is familiar with programs and benefits such as Medicare, Medicaid, SNAP (Food Stamps), Social Security, protective services, legal services, adult day care, housing, transportation, and local senior and community groups. If you receive a letter indicating you are being sent a new Medicare Card and you actually receive a new card, it is a precautionary measure taken by the Center for Medicare & Medical Services (CMS) - IT IS NOT A SCAM. When in doubt, Medicare Recipients should call MEDICARE or engage the Senior Medicare Patrol if they have questions or concerns. Tree Warden The Tree Warden is responsible for the care and control of all trees on public properties in Killingworth, including roadways. If a tree is scheduled to be removed or pruned, the Tree Warden will post a notice on the tree at least five days in advance, unless the tree poses an imminent threat to public safety. If an objection is registered, the Tree Warden will hold a public hearing on the matter and render a decision in writing. The Tree Warden is appointed by the Board of Selectmen for a two - year term pursuant to CT State Statutes. Christopher C. hours: About Welcome to the Town of Litchfield’s official website. I hope it is helpful and informative. In addition to the website, I invite you to visit our town, not only for its rich history, but also for its special annual events such as the Litchfield Hills Road Race, our Town wide Fireworks Community Celebration, Wednesday nights free Concerts on the Green, Scarecrows in the Meadow, the Holiday Stroll and the Milton Resolution Run. We also offer the beauty of open space, lake and waterways for fishing, boating and swimming. Miles of trails offer hiking opportunities from a short waterway boardwalk to day - long treks and horse trails. When it’s time to relax we have a wonderful selection of restaurants, shops and a spacious Green. For the summer of 2024, we look forward to the grand openings of the "Abner" Hotel which will be located inside the historic county Courthouse in the Center of Town, the Belden House & the Mews, just a few steps away located on North Street in the former Rosehaven facility and the transformation of the Old Tollgate Inn reopening as the Lost Fox Inn, located on Rte 202. We’ve worked hard to create a comprehensive website that is easy to navigate and helps you find what you seek. We would appreciate any feedback you would be willing to provide us at (860) 567 - 7550. department: Lisa A. Losee, CCTC Town Clerk & Registrar of Vital Statistics Term: Jan 5, 2026 - Jan 7, 2030
address: 74 West Street, put into the Official Ballot Drop Box at Town Hall, or mailed to PO Box 488, L
hours: About The Town Clerk’s Office is open for regular business Monday through Friday from 9:00 a.m. to 4:30 p.m. The following does not apply to Referendum: Absentee Ballot Voting: Those electors choosing to vote by absentee ballot can: pick up a paper application in person from the Town Clerk’s office request one by emailing townclerk@townoflitchfieldct.gov or by calling the office at 860 - 567 - 7561 may download a fillable application off the town website under Town Clerk Resources Down loadable forms or from the Secretary of the State’s office at https://portal.ct.gov/SOTS/Election - Services/Voter - Information/Absentee - Voting may apply on - line through the Secretary of the State's office at https://oabr - sots.ct.gov/OABR/absenteeBallotReqPortalHome.do Applications must be returned to the Town Clerk’s office either in person at 74 West Street, put into the Official Ballot Drop Box at Town Hall, or mailed to PO Box 488, Litchfield, CT 06759 before a ballot can be issued. Completed Ballots must be received by the Town Clerk's office by the close of the polls on election day in order to be counted. The Official Ballot Drop Box at the rear of the Town Hall will be open and will remain open until 8:00 pm on election day. Land Records: Grantor/Grantee indexes for the Town of Litchfield land recordings are searchable on - line via www.uslandrecords.com dating back to 05/24/1979. Documents may be viewed free of charge. Printing fees do apply. Recordings: Recordings may be made in person or by mail at PO Box 488, or 74 West Street, Litchfield, CT 06759 for the Town of Litchfield only - NO COUNTY RECORDING IN CT . If needed, call in advance at 860 - 567 - 7561. We do not have Judicial Records such as divorce decrees in this office. You must contact the Litchfield Superior Court in Torrington, CT at 860 - 626 - 2300 to purchase copies of those documents Dog Licenses: Dog Licenses must be obtained and renewed during the month of June to avoid late fee penalties A ll Other Concerns please email us at townclerk@townoflitchfieldct.gov or call 860 - 567 - 7561. We will assist you as best as we can. The office of the Town Clerk is one of the oldest positions in government and the duties and responsibilities are vast and numerous. In this office all records of the town are faithfully recorded, filed, maintained and preserved in accordance with State Statute. This includes all land records; maps and surveys; vital records such as births, deaths, marriages and burial records; military discharges; election results; town ordinances; committee and commission memberships, meeting schedules, notices and minutes; trade name certificates; liquor licenses; and notary appointments, to name a few. Many of our records go back to the 1700 and 1800’s and most records are available to the public during regular business hours. In addition to being the official records keeper the Town Clerk is the keeper of the Town Seal; administering oaths, witnessing signatures and affixing the town seal to all official town documents. The Town Clerk serves as Registrar of Vital Statistics, an elections officer responsible for the ordering of election supplies, the issuing and accounting of all absentee ballots & the certification of election results; and as an agent of the State of Connecticut. Other duties include, but are not limited to, the collection of real estate conveyance tax, open space penalties; issuing marriage licenses and certified copies of vital records; appointing unaffiliated JP’s; the annual registration of dogs; the sale of sportsmen’s licenses; and the assistance with voter registration. In the Town of Litchfield the Clerk’s office is the official posting place for agendas and legal notices. Pistol permit applications are found here. The Town Clerk’s office is a source of information for the public, visitors and residents alike. Inquiries are made, questions are answered, research is conducted, and genealogical puzzles are solved. We welcome every opportunity to serve the public. Role of Tax Assessor If you are dropping off information, please use the drop slot and be sure to include a phone number so that we may contact you with any questions. You may also email information to assessor@meridenct.gov . Location: 142 East Main Street, Meriden CT 06450 Phone: 203 - 630 - 4065 Fax 203 - 630 - 4068 Office Hours: Monday - Friday: 8:00 am to 5:00 pm City Assessor: Donna Calicchio Deputy Assessor: Jayleesa Elder 2025 Revaluation In accordance with Connecticut General Statutes, the City of Meriden is engaged in a property revaluation to establish updated Real Estate assessments for the October 1, 2025 Grand List. This revaluation will correspond to the tax bills that will be due beginning in July 2026. To complete this revaluation the City of Meriden has contracted with Vision Government Solutions, a State of Connecticut certified Revaluation Company, to assist the Assessor in completing the revaluation project. Please find all updated information on the 2025 Revaluation Project on our 2025 Citywide Revaluation Page. Primary Duties of the Tax Assessor’s Office The Tax Assessor's Office is responsible for the discovery, listing, valuation and equalization of all properties in the City Of Meriden. This office is also responsible for the maintenance of assessment records and annual calculation of the tax base. This office values all real and personal property located in the City of Meriden on a fair and equitable basis according to Connecticut General Statutes. How your Property is Assessed. The Assessor creates a Grand List of real and personal property owned and located in the City of Meriden as of October 1st each year. To compile the City’s Grand List the Assessor follows these three primary steps: 1. Discover 2. List 3. Value It is the goal of the Assessor to compile a Grand List that is fair and equitable to all property owners, as stipulated by State law. The above steps are taken to ensure that no property owners are assessed more than once for the same property, or omitted from the list in error. The Assessor also makes every attempt to ensure that the correct person or persons are assessed accordingly. The following is a brief description of each step in the assessment process. Questions on the New Property Tax Exemption for Veterans With a P&T Disability Rating By: Jessica Schaeffer - Helmecki, Senior Legislative Attorney July 22, 2024 | 2024 - R - 0124 This report answers questions about the new property tax exemption for veterans who have a service - connected permanent and total (P&T) disability rating ( PA 24 - 46 ), the relevant portion of which is expected to be codified as CGS § 12 - 81(83) ). We address each question separately. The Office of Legislative Research is not authorized to provide legal opinions and this report should not be considered one. Click here for the report DISCOVER The first duty of the Assessor is to discover real and personal property located in the City. The Assessor begins this process with the previous year’s Grand List and utilizes various resources to compile the next year’s Grand List. Some of these resources include the following: 1. City Clerk’s records, including deed transfers and trade name certificates 2. Maps, including survey maps and subdivision maps, by comparing assessment records with the GIS parcel database. 3. Commercial Records 4. Building permits and demolition permits 5. City Fire Marshall reports 6. Newspapers and other periodicals 7. Physical inspections of properties 8. Business Personal Property declarations 9. CT DMV listing of registered vehicles 10. City Health Inspector reports 11. Liquor license applications 12. Secretary of the State 13. Information forwarded from other Assessors 14. Other taxpayers 15. Online databases and resources LIST Once property has been located, the Assessor includes it in the City’s Grand List. Each year’s Grand List is printed and bound into books, categorized by Real Estate, Motor Vehicle and Personal Property. The list is arranged in alphabetical order, by owner last name or business name. The Assessor also maintains property record cards for each parcel of real estate located in the City. These cards include owner name(s) and mailing address, parcel size and location, as well as Assessor map number and Assessor parcel or lot number. In addition, each parcel carries a number that uniquely identifies it. Some or all of this information enables identification of a parcel on the Assessor maps. VALUE All property is valued by the Assessor in a uniform manner and according to the laws of the State of Connecticut. Connecticut General Statutes (12 - 63) state that the present true and actual value of property must be its fair market value. Fair market value is the amount of money for which property may be exchanged within a reasonable time period, and under conditions in which all parties are willing, able, and reasonably well - informed. General Statute (12 - 64) states that all nonexempt property shall be included in the grand list of the city or town in which it is located on October 1. Real and personal property (which includes motor vehicles) is assessed at seventy percent of its market value. Notice is also given a public hearing will be held to review and discuss the contents of the draft CAPER. The CAPER reports on accomplishments and expenditures for the City’s Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) program as required by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD). In PY48, the City was allocated $979, 930 in CDBG annual allocation funds and had $ 367, 531 in Revolving Loan Funds to address the priority needs of low - to moderate - income residents with public facilities and infrastructure improvements, affordable housing, public services, and economic development opportunities. PY48 started on July 1, 2022 and ended on June 30, 2023. PUBLIC COMMENT PERIOD: Citizens are invited to review and make comments on the draft CAPER. During the Public Comment Period, a copy of the draft CAPER is available in the Office of Community Development, Room 134, City Hall, 142 East Main Street, Meriden, CT 06450 as well as on the Community Development website at: https://www.meridenct.gov/government/departments/economic - development/community - development/ . Written comments on the CAPER will be accepted for 15 days beginning October 10, 2023 through October 24, 2023 until 4:30PM EST. PUBLIC HEARING: A public hearing will be held virtually and in - person on October 25, 2023 at 5:00 PM EST . This means citizens will have the ability to meet in person at the Council Chambers, Room 206 at City Hall or online via Microsoft Teams in real time through the internet on a computer or other electronic device via this link: https://tinyurl.com/bddvnkfp . For more information on how to participate at the public hearing please call Jeanette Allam, Community Development & Grants Administrator at (203) 630 - 4151 or email at jallam@meridenct.gov or on the Community Development website as listed above. Accommodations will be made for non - English speakers and/or those with disabilities upon request. Please contact Jeanette Allam, Community Development & Grants Administrator, at the address and/or telephone number listed above at least 24 hours prior to the hearing, if auxiliary aids are required. ROONEY The Town of Monroe is proud to announce that it is ranked top five safest places to live in Connecticut according to national and state statistics. It has a rich history, vibrant community and bright future. Monroe is an old town with new spirit. https://www.onlyinyourstate.com/connecticut/town - safety - ct/ Incorporated in 1823 and named after the fifth President, James Monroe, Monroe celebrated its Bicentennial in 2023. A community rich in history and suburban charm, we welcome everyone to come visit our farmer’s market, summer festivals, outdoor concerts, and community events. Monroe also offers award winning blue ribbon school systems, state championship sports programs, and our nationally recognized robotics teams. Monroe is one of the most desirable places for young families seeking homeownership, work opportunities and a healthy lifestyle. Our family oriented community and close proximity to multiple cities makes Monroe one of the most convenient commuter places to live in Connecticut. While celebrating our waterfront venues, well maintained parks, and our natural trail systems, Monroe offers breathtaking outdoor experiences. The Town of Monroe is committed to enhancing the quality of life for its residents and visitors. Please take a trip to Monroe and see for yourself. You might want to stop into one of our many high quality restaurants or sit at a cafe to have coffee with a friend. You will find our residents and business owners to be warm and welcoming. If you want to learn more about Monroe - email our office here . First Selectman Terrence P. Rooney was first elected in 2023 and is currently serving his 2nd term in office. The First Selectman is elected for a two year term during the municipal election (odd numbered years). As per the Town Charter, The First Selectman is the chief executive officer of the Town and has all of the executive powers vested by law or by Charter, except as otherwise provided or limited by the Town Charter. The First Selectman shall not engage in any other employment during the term of elected office. The duties of the First Selectman include administration of all departments and agencies of the Town, making reports to Town Council as a non - voting participant, approval or veto of Town ordinances, appointing Town electors to boards and commissions, and making final appointments of Town employees except as provided otherwise in the Charter. (The full list of duties is provided in Chapter III of the Town Charter.) Terrence P. hours: The office of the Tax Collector’s Department mission is to serve the public in a courteous and efficient manner while providing the tax collection effort in conformance with applicable state statutes. Primary Programs & Services Collect current and delinquent taxes, representing over 92% of the total revenue for the Town.  Mailing of tax bills, delinquent notices, balance due notices and intent to lien notices. Conduct tax sales.  Process Certificate of Corrections and transfers.  Track escrow and delinquent accounts, bankruptcies, and refunds. Produce and file tax liens.  Interface with DMV, collection agencies, and auditors.  Provide regular reporting to the Board of Finance and Director of Finance.  Coordinate with vendor and Parks and Recreation Department for distribution of park stickers. The Fiscal Year 2025 - 2026 Mill Rate is 28.67 on Real Estate, Personal Property and Motor Vehicles. Tax Payments may be made online for a convenience fee. 100% of all convenience fees go directly to the processing company: Credit/Debit Card Convenience Fee: 2.99% of the payment. Credit/Debit Card Convenience Fee minimum $3.95. e - Check Fee: 95 Cents, regardless of the payment amount. Click Here to View and Pay all Tax Bills Online For more information on your tax payments, please contact the Tax Collector’s Office at 203 - 452 - 2804. Additional Links Wolfe Park Sticker Information Motor Vehicle Value Articles Contact Information Address: Monroe Tax Collector's Office 7 Fan Hill Road Monroe, CT 06468 Hours Monday - Thursday 8:30 to 4:30 Friday 8:30 to 1:30 Tax Collector Deborah Heim, CCMC Email Phone: (203) 452 - 2804

CLICK HERE for details. The purpose of the Town Clerk’s Office is to record, index, and make documents available for public inspection, and to maintain records and vital statistics. Our office is the repository of an enormous amount of information and we administer a variety of services including: Elections Issuance of Licenses and Permits Land Records and Vital Statistics Notary Services List of Justices of the Peace A fee schedule for all records and licensing provided is available online. hours: About First Selectman Tom Weik First Selectman Tom Weik was born and raised in Morris, where he attended James Morris School and Wamogo Regional High School before continuing his education in Boston. He and his wife, Lisa, have three daughters, all of whom attended local schools before pursuing higher education. A dedicated and longtime community volunteer, Tom has served Morris in numerous capacities, including the Sandy Beach Commission, Morris Public Library Board of Directors, Bantam Lake Authority, Bantam Lake Rehabilitation Committee, Bantam Lake Protective Association, Morris Land Trust, Morris Historical Society, Morris Fire Company, and The Reading Room and Museum Committee of the James Morris Library. He also served two terms on the Board of Selectmen prior to being elected First Selectman in 2015. Tom maintains an open - door policy at Town Hall and welcomes the opportunity to speak with residents in person, by phone, or via email. hours: About HOURS Effective Thursday November 20, 2025 New Office Hours: Thursdays 12:00 - 5:00 with the exception of JULY ONLY office is open until 6:00 pm To search for your paid or due taxes go to the Online Lookup . Please contact the Tax Collector’s Office with questions. (860) 567 - 7435 or email for a quicker response. If you are paying your Motor Vehicle taxes late and you need a release for the Department of Motor Vehicles to register any vehicle in your name, payment must be made in Cash or pay online using the information below. No personal or business checks will be accepted. Per Town Ordinance established 1/15/2004. **Email the Tax Collector for a DMV release when office is not open. Meetings Upcoming View All Currently, there are no upcoming meetings posted for this entity. Recently Held View All No items found. Latest News Tax Collectors Notice June 13, 2024 Property Taxes Due - Last day to pay Thursday Feb. 1st - 11am to 4pm January 25, 2024 FAQs Real Estate The only bill you will receive for Real Estate taxes is the bill mailed at the end of June 2018. If the bill is $100.00 or less it is due and payable in full in July 2018. If the bill is over $100.00 it can be paid in two equal installments, half due July 2018 and the other half due January 2019. * Please note that no separate mailing will be sent for the January installment. Real Estate Sold or Transferred Real Estate taxes appear in the name of the owner on the October 1, 2017 Grand List. The new owner’s name(s) will appear on the new grand list’s billing. If property has been sold or transferred, please forward bill to the new owner or to the tax collector. Personal Property The only bill you will receive for Personal Property taxes is the bill mailed at the end of June 2018. If the bill is $100.00 or less it is due and payable in full in July 2018. If the bill is over $100.00 it can be paid in two equal installments, half due July 2018 and the other half due January 2019. * Please note that no separate mailing will be sent for the January installment. Assessments & Exemptions Questions in reference to Assessments & Exemptions should be directed to the Assessor's Office. Any questions in regard to Motor Vehicles which have been sold or registered in another town or state should also be directed to the Assessor's Office, at (860) 567 - 6096. Payments by Mail or Payments in Person If a receipt is desired please send a self - addressed, stamped envelope and the entire bill to the Tax Collector’s Office. If no receipt is desired just send the correct part for the installment you are trying to pay. If you are paying in person, please bring the entire bill. If you have any questions concerning your tax bill, please call the Tax Collector’s Office at (860) 567 - 7435 and we will be happy to assist you. Online Tax Payments Morris taxpayers may pay their Real Estate, Personal Property, Motor Vehicle or Supplemental Motor Vehicle taxes online with a company we have partnered with called Point and Pay. Sewer and/or Deer Island District taxes may NOT be paid or viewed online. If taxes are delinquent, please contact the Tax Office during office hours on Thursdays 11 - 4 at 860.567.7435 for the current amount due prior to making your payment. As an added note, the Tax Office has tried to have all taxes paid and/or due online. Please remember that if your account has been sent for further collections more fees may be added. Credit and MasterCard Debit Card Payments: Online payments can be made using your MasterCard debit or credit card. Point and Pay charges a 2.5% convenience fee with a $2.00 minimum charge for payment processing. Please click on the link below to make an online payment. "Visa Bank Debit" Card Payments: Online payments can be made using your debit card. Point and Pay charges a convenience fee of $3.95 for payment processing. Please click on the link below to make an online payment. ACH Payments: Online payments can be made using your checking or savings account. You will need your routing and account number to use this service. To avoid delays, please double check the account information as you enter it. There is a convenience fee of $2.00 for each payment that is less than or equal to $10, 000.00 and $10.00 for each payment that is greater than $10, 000.00. Please click on the link below to make an online payment. Tax Payments Online (If searching by your name, you must enter in your last name and at least the first initial of your first name of the owner of the property.) The Tax Collector's Office is responsible for collection of payments for real estate, personal property and motor vehicle taxes and for special town services such as the Morris Sewer Authority and the Deer Island Association District Tax. The Tax Collector’s Office is located in the Morris Town Offices in Community Hall and is open to the public during office hours only. The Tax Office takes great pride in the efficiency with which tax matters are handled. The office is responsible for thorough accounting of all records and funds administered. A courteous attitude is mandatory while upholding high standards of ethical conduct and operating in accordance with all applicable federal and state laws. The Town of Morris’ October 1, 2013 Grand List mill rate is 22.38 mills. This means that for every $1, 000 of assessment you pay $22.38. Example: If your motor vehicle is assessed at $1, 500 your taxes would be $33.57. The Deer Island Association October 1, 2013 Grand List mill rate is 2.77 mills. The Morris Sewer Authority has set the sewer user fee for the fiscal year 2014 - 2015 is $300 per user unit. Motor Vehicle If your motor vehicle was sold, repossessed, totaled, donated, stolen, registered out of state, or taxed in the wrong town, please contact the Assessor’s Office. Motor Vehicles that were registered on October 1, 2021 will be due in full in July 2022. Motor Vehicles registered between October 2, 2021 and September 30, 2022 will be due and payable January 2023. Those supplemental bills will be mailed out in December of 2022. If you need to change your mailing address and/or tax town at the DMV, please go to the following link: DMV Address Change Form. You can then mail the completed form to DMV at 60 State Street, Wethersfield, CT 06161 - 5070. We cannot change your address here in the tax office. Please remember that if you fail to do this not only will you not receive future tax bills, but your registration renewal will not be forwarded by the Post Office. If you need to file a lost or stolen plate receipt to cancel a registration please go to the following link: Lost or Stolen Plate and mail it into DMV at 60 State Street, Wethersfield, CT 06161 - 5070. If you are paying your Motor Vehicle taxes late and you need a release for the Department of Motor Vehicles to register any vehicle in your name, payment must be made in Cash. No personal or business checks will be accepted. Per Town Ordinance established 1/15/2004. hours: The town clerk's office is responsible for the recording of all documents pertaining to land transactions. Walk - in land recordings must be processed 15 minutes prior to our closing time . Documents received after this time will be held for recording on the next business day. eRecording land recordings are digitally accepted during business hours between 8:30 a.m. and 3:00 p.m. These documents will not be accepted by our land recordings vendor, Cott Systems, outside of this time slot and must be submitted the following business day. Copies of land records or property descriptions are available for a fee of $1.00 for each page. If you need a certified copy, the cost is an additional $2.00. FEMA Flood Insurance Rate Maps *** Land Parcel Consolidation Procedures / Examples *** Important Land Records Information DRS Releases New CT Real Estate Conveyance Tax Return As an Online Fillable Form Work from Home or Office - Connecticut Town Clerks Portal GIS Mapping Restrictive Covenants e - Recording Now Available Fee Schedule for Recording Documents Maps DRS Releases New CT Real Estate Conveyance Tax Return As an Online Fillable Form The Connecticut Department of Revenue Services has released a new fillable OP - 236 form available online for real estate conveyance tax purposes. The fillable form includes the town clerk's copy and is approved for immediate use. Any remaining OP - 236 carbon paper forms will still be accepted by town clerks and the DRS until the supply is exhausted. For more information, review AN 2016(7), Major Revision to the Connecticut Real Estate Conveyance Tax Return . The new fillable form will improve processing time by being typed and more legible, while the functionality built into the new form will reduce errors and provide for a more complete return. Download the following instructions and forms on the Connecticut Department of Revenue website . OP - 236, Connecticut Real Estate Conveyance Tax Return OP - 236 Schedule A - Grantors, Supplemental Information for Real Estate Conveyance Tax Return OP - 236 Schedule B - Grantees, Supplemental Information for Real Estate Conveyance Tax Return AU - 263 - Real Estate Conveyance Tax Allocation Worksheet Work from Home or Office - Connecticut Town Clerks Portal Portal Access Connecticut Town Clerks Portal Flyer Connecticut Town Clerks Portal Press Release Access and print New Britain land records 24/7 using the Connecticut town clerks portal. Access land records from over 70 cities and towns. New Britain land records index and images from October 1, 1958 to the present are now available. As a subscriber, you get unlimited access to all participating towns under one simple subscription plan. Images prior to October 1958 are available using the historic land records index . These images are not grantor/grantee indexed but are searchable by Volume 1 - 54 (1850 - 1958). Using RecordHub, enter HLR in the search field. All volumes prior to October 1958 will display. Select the volume. The entire volume will open. This will take a moment to load all images from that book. Find the page(s) you wish to view, determine the print range, and print out those records. All town clerk retained New Britain maps are indexed and images are available using the maps index. GIS Mapping New Britain's GIS property mapping application allows you to check maps or color photos of property lines, hydrants, sewers, and more. Restrictive Covenants Connecticut Public Act 21 - 173 defines an unlawful restrictive covenant as " a covenant or other provision in an instrument affecting the title to real property that purports to restrict ownership or occupancy of such real property on the basis of race." This act declares a restrictive covenant void . Pursuant to Public Act 21 - 173, the office of policy and management is providing the restrictive covenant form and restrictive covenant affidavit . It is up to the existing property owner(s), if they choose, to record either of these documents on their land records. Download either the above form or affidavit, complete the document and bring to the town clerk's office for recording. There is no fee for the recording. e - Recording Now Available File your land recording documents electronically from anywhere in the world. Inquire with any of our four delivery agents: Corporate Service Company (CSC), eRecording Partners Network (ePN), Indecomm Global Services or Simplifile to set up your account. Once set up, we will provide you with a submitter ID number. It's fast, easy, convenient, and cost - effective. Email any of our delivery agents for more information. CSC ePN Indecomm Simplifile Fee Schedule for Recording Documents These fees, effective July 1, 2025, include foreclosure registration forms. $70.00 first page, $5.00 each subsequent page or portion $2.00 each transfer reportable to the state of Connecticut $5.00 if current mailing address of grantee is not given $1.00 if names of those executing, witnessing, and acknowledging are not typed or printed under signature $1.00 for each marginal notation of assignment - subsequent to the first two Municipal conveyance tax rate (effective 2/17/2004) is - .005 Maps Map Filing Requirements Maps must contain a red live seal or live stamp of the design professional on mylar material. Bring the mylar map(s) to the city planner prior to filing in the town clerk's office. The city planner will determine whether the map is a subdivision or not. Once the city planner determines the type of map you can submit the map(s) to the town clerk's office for filing. See our Basic Fee Schedule for map charges. Weber CMC Town Clerk & Registrar of Vital Statistics What We Do The Town Clerk is an elected official who serves as a resource for others within the community by providing direction, guidance, information and assistance. The Town Clerk often provides the professional link between residents, local governing bodies and State agencies, and she is also the official keeper of the Town Seal. The Town's Charter and the Connecticut General Statutes define the Town Clerk's duties and functions. The Town Clerk is an elected official who is responsible for the preservation and safekeeping of the Town's official records and documents. Deeds, mortgages, liens, assignments, survey maps, and other instruments concerning title to land are recorded, indexed and microfilmed. Official notices of meetings and most agendas are posted here for public inspection. Original records, which are safeguarded in the Town Clerk's vault, date back to 1801 when New Canaan was incorporated as a town. The meeting records of boards and commissions are kept on file, notaries public are sworn in, trade names and military discharges are recorded, and a number of other permits and licenses are sold through the Town Clerk's Office. The Town Clerk also issues dog licenses which need to be renewed annually in June. The Town Clerk serves as the Town's Registrar of Vital Statistics and issues birth, marriage and death certificates in addition to handling other matters pertaining to vital records. The Town Clerk also issues dog licenses. As a chief election official, the Town Clerk registers new voters, is responsible for the execution of the absentee ballot process, the execution of referenda, and for the calling of a recanvass when a vote is close. Other responsibilities include administering training sessions for poll workers in conjunction with the Registrars of Voters, issuing and certifying the Town's final election results, administering the oath of office to all elected and appointed officials, and keeping the Secretary of State apprised on the status of all Town officials. Local candidates and political campaign committees register with the Town Clerk, who provides information concerning campaign finance guidelines to political parties and individuals seeking public office. People from all political parties, special interest groups, and others regularly request information and guidance from the Town Clerk. The Town Clerk and her staff are committed to providing that information in an impartial, friendly, and helpful way. And if we don't know an answer, we'll do our very best to help you find one! Welcome! hours: Tax Collector's Office Use this link For All Online Payments & For Payment History Please be advised an additional fee is added to use ONLINE bill paying services . If you are in need of immediate Motor Vehicle Clearance payment must be made in the office and CAN NOT be paid online. Online payments could take up to a week to clear. DUE DATES JULY 1st for: 1ST INSTALLMENT OF THE REAL ESTATE & PERSONAL PROPERTY BILLS AND ALL MOTOR VEHICLE BILLS Payments need to be postmarked or received by August 1st to avoid interest. JANUARY 1st for: 2nd Installment OF THE: Real Estate & Personal Property Bills and Supplemental Motor Vehicle Bills Payments need to be postmarked or received by February 1st to avoid interest. *** TAX COLLECTION NOTICE*** . JANUARY 2026 Notice is hereby given to the taxpayers of the Town of New Milford that the first installment of the Real Estate, Personal Property taxes and Motor Vehicle Taxes on the October 1, 2024 Grand List becomes due and payable on January 1, 2026. The new bills will be available online January 1, 2026 at www.mytaxbill.net/newmilford Payment must be received or postmarked by February 2, 2026 to avoid an interest penalty. Per State Statute 12 - 146, interest will be charged at the rate of 1.5% per month from the original due date of January 1, 2026. The minimum interest charge is $2.00. FAILURE TO RECEIVE A TAX BILL DOES NOT INVALIDATE THE TAX OR INTEREST. For your convenience, taxes may be paid in the following ways: Mail - Current payments only during the collection period may be mailed directly to our Bank’s Lockbox Service at the following address: Town of New Milford, PO Box 150416, Hartford, CT 06115 - 0416 in January and July only. Once delinquent, all mail must be sent to Tax Collector's Office, 10 Main Street, New Milford, CT 06776 Webster Bank - ONLY Webster Bank's New Milford branch location will accept tax payments during business hours during the collection period. Only current tax bills and full payments are accepted. There is no charge for this service and you do not have to be a Webster Bank customer. Taxpayers interested in this option must bring the entire tax bill so that both the town and taxpayer portions can be stamped paid as a receipt. If you do not bring the entire bill the bank may not be able to process your payment! Taxpayer’s Bank - t axpayers can pay using their own financial institution’s ‘electronic bill pay’ service at their own risk . Payment must be received by the deadline or interest penalties will apply . To ensure timely payment delivery, taxpayers are encouraged to process their transaction at least 10 days before the due date to ensure timely delivery. If there is no postmark timeliness depends on receipt of the payment. Credit Card - payments can be made in person at the Town of New Milford’s Tax Collector’s office with a 2% surcharge. Mastercard, Visa, Discover and American Express are now accepted in person and also online. Online Payment - mytaxbill.net/newmilford . Paying from a checking account, the fee is only 50¢. Paying by debit/credit card the fee is 2.75%. Note : Taxpayers with delinquent motor vehicle taxes that have been reported to the Department of Motor Vehicles will be assessed a $5 fee per name for processing reporting of the delinquency and payment. Please be advised that the Town of New Milford will use any enforcement tool available by Connecticut State Statute to pursue and collect on delinquent taxes. Also - collection of delinquent bills may be handled by an attorney or collection agency with additional fees added. Timely payments are encouraged to avoid penalties or the possibility of being included in the next Tax Sale. Nancy E. hours: Overview The Tax Collector is an appointed position responsible for collecting real estate, motor vehicle and personal property taxes due the Town. Norfolk taxpayers may pay their Real Estate, Personal Property, Motor Vehicle, or supplemental Motor Vehicle taxes online with Point and Pay, a company the town has partnered with. If taxes are delinquent, please contact the Tax Office during office hours on Tuesday and Thursday from 8:30 a.m. to 12:00 p.m. in person or by phone at 860 - 542 - 5140 for the current amount due prior to making your payment. As an added note, the Tax Office does have all delinquent taxes that are due online. Please remember that, if your account has been sent for further collections, more fees may be added. The Tax Collector’s office is located in Town Hall, 19 Maple Avenue Due to changes in the US Postal Service, mail can no longer be guaranteed to be postmarked on the day it is collected. The US Postal Service has stated that it can now take up to 3 - 4 days for regularly collected mail to be postmarked. It is recommended that anyone mailing in tax payments close to the due dates to hand the letter to the postal worker behind the counter, and they can stamp it with the postmarked date. Doing this will prevent tax bills from being considered late and incurring extra costs and payments. Read more. APPOINTED OFFICIAL & STAFF Pamela D. hours: Town Clerk & Registrar of Vital Statistics Overview The Town Clerk’s office is located in Town Hall, 19 Maple Avenue. The Town Clerk’s office is the official State recording agency for the Town. This includes vital statistics (marriage, birth, death) records, dog licenses and all recording information related to ownership of real property. All town committee minutes and agendas are available to the public. Responsibilities include: Absentee Ballots Marriage Licenses (by appointment only) Dog Licenses, Fishing Licenses Tobey Pond & Transfer Station Permits The Town Clerk is an elected position, serving a four year term. The Town Clerk's Office The Town Clerk’s Office helps residents access important public records and town services. This includes recording land documents, issuing marriage licenses, dog licenses, fishing & hunting permits, providing certified copies of vital records (birth, death, marriage), and keeping the official archive of Town decisions and filings. The office also supports local elections and works to make voting and public participation secure and transparent. We are committed to maintaining North Canaan’s historical records and making town government accessible to everyone. hours: Page MEnu Applications Codebooks Fees & Requirements Pool Installations Supplement To 2018 CT Building Code Home Departments Building Official Attention! Building Permits are available on - line with our new PermitLink Program. You can also pay for the permit on - line. For inspections, you may call 860 - 230 - 3012. Please tell us the type of inspection, owner's name and street address in your message. Construction Plan Requirements As part of the Building Permit Application, a detailed construction plan must be submitted to the Building Inspector before the Building Permit can be issued. The reason for this procedure is to assure that the project you are planning will meet the current State of Connecticut Building Code. If you were to build a project without proper plan review, your project may be rejected and this would surely cost extra time and money. Following is a list of items that should be included on the building plan: Draw the plan neatly and to scale (1/4" per foot etc.) Show a plan view or overhead showing size of building with doors and window openings. Label each room as to the use of that room. Show a cross section or a side view of the foundation type that will be used in this project. Show a cross section or elevation of any wall section showing floor joist, studs, wall plates, ceiling joists rafters and roofing materials. Show a detail of any guardrails and handrail systems at landings, decks and stairways. Any bedroom addition must show an egress size window in each of the bedrooms. What are you doing? This is what you'll need: If you are a homeowner doing work on the single family home you live in: A detailed construction plan (see above) A completed Worker's Compensation Exemption Form 7A (https://portal.ct.gov/ - /media/WCC/forms/building - permit - forms/7a.pdf) If you are hiring someone: A copy of the contractor's license or Home Improvement Registration. A copy of worker's compensation insurance certificate or a completed Worker's Compensation Exemption Form 7A, 7B or 7C. https://portal.ct.gov/WCC/Workers - Compensation - Forms/Building - Permit - Forms Changing electric service: The above contractor information. A Customer Request for Service (CRS) number from Eversource. New project, to include building a shed, garage, deck, sun room, pool, etc: The above contractor information. A B100a permit from NDDH if you have your own septic system and well, a B51d if you have city sewer and your own well. If you are on city sewer and city water, you do not need to contact the Northeast District Department of Health. An approved zoning permit. Detailed plans (see the plan handout). *Detached garages or sheds less than 600 square feet do not require a concrete footing and foundation 42" below grade. Detached sheds under 200 sq. ft. do not require a building permit. They do require a zoning permit and possibly a Northeast District Dept of Health B110a or B51d form. If you are replacing a deck, pool, porch, shed, etc: The above contractor information. Under most conditions, the requirements are the same as a new project. New home or addition: The above contractor information. A driveway permit for a new home. Septic system or sewer permit. Zoning permit. If the building will be heated, you must show compliance with the 2015 International Energy Code. See your builder or ask for a guideline handout. Need Codebooks? hours: Related Links Online Bill Payments CT Tax Sales Fire District Taxes Pending Tax Sales of Certain CT Municipalities Home Departments Tax Collector's Office About the Tax Collector The Tax Collector is responsible for the collection of all monies due to the Town including, but not limited to: Real Estate Taxes, Sewer Use, Sewer Assessment, Motor Vehicle Taxes, and Personal Property Taxes. The Collector is also responsible for reporting fiscal information to the various Town Departments, Boards and Commissions, the State of Connecticut and the United States Bureau of the Census Payment Methods The office accepts checks, cash, money orders, and credit cards . To pay your taxes online. use this LINK with a credit card, debit card or electronic check. There is a fee charged for the use of this service which is applied at the time of your transaction: Credit Cards: 2.5%, $2.00 Minimum Debit card: $3.95 E - Checks: Flat fee of $2.00 on checks up to $10, 000. $10 fee on anything over $10, 000 Interest is assessed on all delinquent amounts at the first of the month at the rate of 1.5% per month, from the DUE DATE. There is a minimum interest charge of $2 per bill. Please note that to obtain Sewer Usage information, you must use the property's Unique ID number. (This is important as both Real Estate Taxes and Sewer Use come out on the same bill here so must be included in an escrow) . You can find the 8 - Digit Unique ID number from your Real Estate tax bill, as highlighted below. Disclaimer: The information obtained is provided as a courtesy to our taxpayers. If using this service for income tax purposes we strongly advise taxpayers to contact a professional Federal Income Tax Preparer or the Internal Revenue Service for assistance in properly utilizing the information provided. In the gathering and preparation of the information provided, every effort has been made to provide the most current information. Nonetheless, inadvertent errors can occur, and applicable laws, rules and regulations often change. The lawful recipient of the displayed information is therefore advised to verify the displayed information before relying on it. Projected Tax Change Calculator Enter the assessment and the mill rate from two different years to compare. Before Assessment: Mill Rate: Property Tax: $ After Assessment Mill Rate: Property Tax: Assessment Change: = Mill Rate Change: = Projected Tax Change: = FIRE DISTRICT TAXES Fire district taxes are NOT collected in this office. To pay your fire taxes, please go directly to the fire department of your village. See the link below for contact information of the fire districts: Information for Fire District Taxes Hours & Location 8 Community Ave. Plainfield, CT 06374 Monday - Wednesday: 8:00am - 5:00pm Thursday: 8:00am - 6:00pm Fridays: CLOSED CONTACT US Karen Vincent, CCMC Tax Collector E - mail: kvincent@plainfieldct.org 860 - 230 - 3019 Linda Gasiorek, CCMC Assistant Tax Collector E - mail: lgasiorek@plainfieldct.org 860 - 230 - 30 18 Direct FAX phone number: 860 - 230 - 0295 Mailing Address: P.O. Box 202 Plainfield, CT 06374 Frequently Asked Questions What if I do not receive a bill? If you do not receive a tax bill for a vehicle you own, contact the Tax Office (860) 230 - 3019 Connecticut State Statute 12 - 130 states that failure to receive a bill does not invalidate the tax. How is the tax rate established? A property tax rate is expressed in mills, or thousandth of a dollar. Tax rate (mill rate) of 28.33 mills is equivalent to $28.33 in taxes per $1000 of net assessed value. The Town of Plainfield sets the mill rate annually in May/June as part of a municipal budget process. What happens if I no longer own the motor vehicle or have moved out of Plainfield? Contact the Assessor's office (860) 230 - 3008 What happens if my taxes become delinquent? A: Interest is assessed on all delinquent amounts at the first of the month at the rate of 1.5% per month, from the DUE DATE. There is a minimum interest charge of $2 per bill.

Office of the Building Department Contact Info 33 East Main Street - 1st floor Portland Connecticut ​ ​ David Smith Building Official Phone: 860 - 342 - 6728 Faye Yacka Administrative Assistant Phone: 860 - 342 - 6727 Fax: 860 - 342 - 6787 Office Hours Monday, Wednesday, Thursday 8:30 a.m. - 4:30 p.m. Tuesday 8:30 a.m. - 7:00 p.m. Friday 8:30 a.m. - 12:00 p.m. Land Use Department Blight Complaint Form Contractor Information Form Inland Wetlands Form Planning & Zoning App. & Fees Residential Solar (PV) Permit Process Supplemental Form Zoning Board of Appeals App. Form Seasonal Information Snow and Ice Wood Stoves How To Land Use New Home Building Package Deck Permit Application Directions Shed Permit Application Directions Pool Permit Application Directions Online Permit Portal We are pleased to announce the launch of our online permit portal on April 13, 2026. Beginning on this date, all permit applications must be submitted electronically. Paper applications will no longer be accepted. SmartGov Building Starting January 1, 2026, we are no longer issuing blanket permits. All permits must be submitted and paid for individually. The Building Department administers and enforces the Connecticut State Building Code* for new construction, renovations, alterations, remodeling, and demolition. Services include plan review and approvals, required inspections throughout construction, system testing, and the issuance of Certificates of Occupancy. The Building Official ensures compliance with state statutes to protect life safety, property, and long - term community investments. The Building Code prioritizes occupant safety, structural integrity, and protection during emergency and extreme weather events. Property owners and design professionals are encouraged to meet with the Land Use and Building Department early in the planning process to review applicable codes and regulations. The Building Department works closely with other town departments to guide projects through the regulatory process. The Building Official also oversees enforcement of the Town’s Blight Ordinance, which addresses the visible accumulation of non - usable items, vehicles, and construction debris to protect neighborhood appearance and property values. Building construction is one of the nation’s major economic indicators and is often a homeowner’s or business’s most significant lifetime investment. As a result, it can be a very stressful process. Prior to finalizing your plans, we strongly encourage property owners and their design professionals to meet with the Land Use and Building Department for a pre - review of the project. This early review helps evaluate how applicable codes and regulations may affect the proposal. Building Permit Requirements The Portland Building Department requires a building permit for the construction or alteration of any structure or building, in accordance with the Connecticut State Building Code and the approved plans and specifications submitted with the application. Items that require a permit may include, but are not limited to: Construction of buildings, additions, and renovations Installation of new siding Replacement of windows Construction of accessory garages and sheds (over 200 sq.ft.) Installation of pools and hot tubs Construction of decks and porches Installation of new roofs or re - roofs when more than 25% of the roof area is affected Replacement of boilers, furnaces, or water heaters Additions of or modifications to heating systems Installation or replacement of air conditioning systems Addition, relocation, or replacement of plumbing fixtures Installation of new electrical circuits or changes to electrical services Installation of roof - mounted or ground - mounted solar panels Permit Fees Permit fees are based on the estimated total cost of the work, including labor and materials. Fees are calculated at $25.00 for the first thousand and $15.00 for each thousand thereafter. ​ Inspections By submitting an application, the applicant(s) and/or owner(s) agree that the Town of Portland, its departments, or their agent(s) are authorized to enter upon the property for which this permit applies for the purpose of inspection and enforcement and administration for the Town of Portland. This permit is issued based upon the plot plan and all supporting documents submitted. Falsification by misrepresentation or omission or failure to comply with the conditions of this permit shall constitute a violation and shall render this permit void. Please click the image to view in larger detail Personal Property 2025 Declaration of Personal Property Form Renters Rebate Program Renters Rebate Program Form Assessment 4General Assessment Information The annual Grand List or Assessment Date is October 1st. In compliance with Connecticut General Statutes, the Town of Prospect has completed a real property revaluation as required for the October 1, 2024, Grand List. The first tax bills to reflect the revised assessments will be issued on or about July 1, 2025. The new 2024 Revaluation assessments can be found by going to eQuality Valuation Service Tax Relief/Credits Local Tax Credits for the Elderly and Totally Disabled 2025 Information Application for Tax Credits (Elderly and Totally Disabled Homeowner) Local Elderly Application FOR NEW APPLICANTS: The filing period for tax credits for Elderly and Totally Disabled Homeowners for the 2025 Grand List is from February 1, 2026, through May 15, 2026. To apply, the applicant must meet age, income limits ($46, 300.00 FOR SINGLE & $56, 500 FOR MARRIED), and own and occupy the home. He or she must have been 65 years of age by December 31, 2025, or be totally and permanently disabled before reaching the age of 65. All income must be documented. The maximum income for the local program for single applicants is $69, 450.00, and for married applicants is $84, 750.00. Applicants must show proof of all income received for the 2025 calendar year by supplying a copy of their Federal Income Tax Return. If you do not need to file a return, bring all year - end statements and the 2025 Social Security Benefit Statement (Form SSA - 1099). Totally disabled applicants must submit current written proof of their disability and income. Proof of disability can include Social Security, Federal, State, or Local government retirement or disability plan, Railroad Retirement Act, or government - related teacher's retirement plan. The maximum credit under the Town program is $400. It is also based upon the percentage of ownership and years as a resident of Prospect. An applicant who meets all requirements and has resided in Prospect for 1 - 5 years is eligible for $200; if an applicant meets the requirements and has lived in Prospect for 6 or more years, they would be eligible for $400. To apply for the tax credit, the applicant, a relative, or an agent must come to the Prospect Assessor's Office before the filing period ends on May 15, 2026. Please call the Prospect Assessor's Office for an appointment or come to the office during normal business hours of 8:30 am to 4:00 pm.

An applicant who meets all requirements and has resided in Prospect for 1 - 5 years is eligible for $200; if an applicant meets the requirements and has lived in Prospect for 6 or more years, they would be eligible for $400. To apply for the tax credit, the applicant, a relative, or an agent must come to the Prospect Assessor's Office before the filing period ends on May 15, 2026. Please call the Prospect Assessor's Office for an appointment or come to the office during normal business hours of 8:30 am to 4:00 pm. To accomplish this objective, our office must discover, list, and equitably value all property within the Town of Redding. In addition, our office is also responsible for the administration of various forms of tax relief. The result of these efforts is the Grand List: a record of all taxable and exempt property in the Town of Redding. Property is always assessed as of October 1. Three classes of property are subject to taxation: real estate, motor vehicle, and business personal property. Real estate includes land and improvements that are permanently affixed to the land. Motor vehicles are any vehicles registered to the Town of Redding between October 1 and September 30 of any given year. Personal property is all property not classified as real property, including machinery, equipment, furniture, fixtures, unregistered motor vehicles, and farming tools. Personal property owned by business entities is taxable in the State of Connecticut. Property is taxed on an ‘ad valorem’ basis, which means ‘at value’. All property is taxed at 70% of fair market value as of October 1 of any given year. Should you have any questions, please call 203 - 938 - 2626. The Net Taxable Grand List total for October 1, 2025 is: Real Estate $1, 763, 988, 390 Motor Vehicle $109, 503, 410 Personal Property $87, 969, 699 Net Total: $1, 961, 461, 499 2027 TOWN - WIDE REVALUATION In accordance with Connecticut General Statutes, the Town of Redding is conducting a real property revaluation as required for the Grant List of October 1, 2027. This revaluation will correspond to the tax bills that will be due in July 2028. property information will be obtained from inspections, data mailers, building permits and sales data. They are First Selectwoman Julia Pemberton (D), Selectwoman Diana Carlino (D), and Selectman Michael Thompson (R). Our Board of Selectmen carries out the acts, policies, and ordinances of the Town Meeting and “superintend the concerns of the Town, adjust and settle all claims against the Town, and draw orders on the Treasurer for their payment.” The Selectmen appoint members of the Board of Ethics and the Park and Recreation Department. Tax Collector The Office of the Tax Collector is responsible for the billing and collection of property taxes, including Real Estate, Motor Vehicle, and Business Personal Property, which accounts for roughly 95% of the Town’s operating budget. We take great pride in the efficiency with which tax matters are handled. This office is responsible for thorough accounting of all records and funds administered. We uphold high standards of ethical conduct and always operate in accordance with the General Statutes of Connecticut and local Town ordinances. The Office of the Tax Collector also oversees the billing and collection of sewer usage charges on the 77 parcels of property connected to the Georgetown Sewer System. Meter readings are provided by Aquarion Water Company and Veolia Water North America, and usage charges are calculated by Tax Office personnel on a quarterly basis. Tax bills for the upcoming fiscal year are mailed the last week of June in the current fiscal year. Tax bills contain the July and January payment stubs. The first half payment is due July 1, 2025. The last day to pay without penalty is August 1, 2025. The second half payment is due January 1, 2026. The last day to pay without penalty is February 1, 2026. Please contact us with any questions you may have; we welcome the opportunity to speak with you. Taxes may be paid online by paying here . Please note, if you need to register a new vehicle or renew an existing registration, taxes MUST be paid with a credit card (not debit card or E - check). Please call the Office at 203 - 938 - 2706 once you have completed the transaction for immediate clearance. Mill Rate History The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2025/26 is 29.54. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2024/25 is 28.72. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2023/24 was 27.69. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2022/23 was as follows: 33.34 for real estate and personal property 32.46 for motor vehicle The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2021/22 was 32.84. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2020/21 was 32.84. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2019/20 was 32.84. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2018/19 was 31.72. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2017/18 was 29.62. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2016/17 was 29.24. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2015/16 was 28.91. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2014/15 was 28.91. The Mill Rate for Fiscal Year 2013/14 was 28.95. hours: Home | Government | Town Administration | Town Clerk Town Clerk’s Office The Town Clerk keeps all of the Town’s records, including agendas and minutes of meetings. She records all deeds, mortgages, and other instruments affecting property, including maps and transfers. The Clerk acts as Registrar of Vital Statistics and Custodian of the Seal of Redding. She retains on file all Town ordinances and copies of the Connecticut General Statutes. She prepares ballots, accepts filings of candidates for election, and records all votes. The Town Clerk serves as the Clerk of the Town Meeting. The Town Clerk’s office is a great source of historical information about Redding. In addition to researching land records, you can pick up a copy of the Town’s Annual Report or purchase historical volumes such as Daniel Cruson’s Images of America: Redding and Easton . Custodian of the Seal of Redding: The Town Clerk acts as Custodian of the Seal of Redding when she applies the seal to all official documents pertaining to the Town. Absentee Ballots: Absentee Ballots for all referendums, primaries, and elections are issued by the Town Clerk’s Office. For information on applying for an absentee ballot, visit this page . Land Records: All property transfers, mortgages, assignments, liens, releases, maps, etc., are recorded and maintained by the Town Clerk. Indexes and images dating back to January 21, 1969, are currently available online at Search IQS . Guest users can view index information and images (if available) at no charge. Images can be printed for $1.50 per page. Trade names are viewable from January 1, 2006, to the current date. Fraud Alert Service: Property owners can sign up for notifications when anything is recorded under their name(s). Please remember that there may be other property owners in town that have the same name. Here’s how to sign up: Go to Search IQs Fraud Alert and enter your email and names to be alerted on (up to 3). Check your email and click the link confirming your sign - up. You will then receive email alerts for any documents that are recorded in the future. Trade Name Certificates To file a Trade Name Certificate, complete the appropriate form ( view the form ), sign in the presence of a Notary, and bring or mail it to the Town Clerk’s office. The fee to file a Trade Name Certificate is $20.00. Notarization is also available for an additional $5.00. We accept cash and checks only. Registrar of Vital Statistics: The Town Clerk acts as Registrar of Vital Statistics when she issues marriage licenses and records all births, marriages, and deaths. To obtain a certified copy of a birth, marriage, or death certificate, visit this page for complete information. Notary Public: Notary services are available in the Town Clerk’s office during office hours. The fee is $5.00 per document. Please note: We cannot notarize wills, trusts, or any documents to be recorded on the land records. Dog Licenses: Per CT General Statute Sec. 22 - 338, all dogs over the age of 6 months must be licensed by the Town Clerk annually on or before June 30th. The license is valid from July 1st through June 30th. Licenses will not be issued without proof of current rabies vaccination. Visit this page for more information and the Dog License Application. “Be safe, be legal - license your dogs by June 30th!” Town Clerk Email Michele R. hours: Assessor Assessor: Kayla Johnson Email: kjohnson@salisburyct.us Assistant Assessor: JoAnne Dodge Email: jdodge@salisburyct.us Hours: Monday, Wednesday, Friday 9:00am - 4:00pm ** Phone Number: 860 - 435 - 5176 ** Starting December 5th, 2025 the Town Hall will be CLOSING at 3:00pm on Fridays The physical location, at 27 Main Street in Salisbury, is in the corner, to the right of the first floor entrance. The Assessor is a town employee, professionally educated, state certified and responsible for the appraisal of all real estate, motor vehicles and certain types of personal property as required by Connecticut State Law. The money needed to operate the town, including the public school, road maintenance, recreation, police and other services, comes primarily from property taxes. Individual tax bills are the property assessment (70% of value) multiplied by the mill rate. The mill rate is determined each year by dividing the budget needed to operate the town by the taxable grand list. Each and every property must be properly assessed so that taxes are apportioned fairly. Every five years the Assessor is required to revaluate all property and prepare an updated grand list (a total assessment of all listed properties) upon which the taxes are based. Our FIELD CARDS are now ONLINE!!!! https://www.propertyrecordcards.com/SearchMaster.aspx?towncode=122 TOWN OF SALISBURY CONNECTICUT GIS & Real Property Information https://terrenogis.com/?viewer=cama/salisburyct Press Release: The Town of Salisbury Assessor’s Office has contracted with eQuality Valuations to complete the state mandated revaluation. The revaluation will be effective on the October 1, 2025 Grand List. The last reval was completed for the October 1, 2020 Grand List. The October 1, 2025 valuations will be reflected in the July 1, 2026 tax bills. If you have any questions, please contact the assessor’s office at assessor@salisburyct.us . Valuation notices will be mailed by eQuality in the third week of December. Once you receive your notice, if you have questions, you must reach out to eQuality directly to set up a phone hearing. This information and instructions will be included in the Assessment notice. After hearings are complete, new notices will be issued where applicable. Applications to the Board of Assessment Appeals will be available on the town website once the Grand List is completed. DO NOT use the current mill rate to estimate taxes. The mill rate will change, and will be determined by the Board of Finance in the late spring of 2026. name: Land Use
title: Abby Conroy, Director of Land Use
hours: Zoning Board of Appeals IMPORTANT NOTICE: Beware of Ongoing Scam Attempts What’s happening: We’ve been made aware of fraudulent messages (via email) going out to applicants claiming to require additional fees for an application to be approved. What you should know: o The Town will never ask for payments via wire transfer. o All email communications will come from our verified channels, such as landuse@salisburyct.us, or via OpenGov.com. o If you receive any suspicious communication, please do not click on any links or provide any information. Instead, please reach out to us separately via a known channel to verify that the communication you received is legitimate. What to do if you’re contacted: 1. Do not respond. 2. Take a screenshot or copy the message. 3. Report it to us by emailing it to landuse@salisburyct.us We take your security seriously and are actively working on protecting the Town from these scams. Please remain vigilant and contact us if you have any concerns. Thank you for your continued trust.
